elements.sent-2411-001: Conformance testing of automata is about checking the equivalence of a known specification and a black-box implementation.
elements.sent-2411-002: An important notion in conformance testing is that of a complete test suite, which guarantees that if an implementation satisfying certain conditions passes all tests, then it is equivalent to the specification.
elements.sent-2411-003: We introduce a framework for proving completeness of test suites at the general level of automata in monoidal closed categories.
elements.sent-2411-004: Moreover, we provide a generalization of a classical conformance testing technique, the W-method.
elements.sent-2411-005: We demonstrate the applicability of our results by recovering the W-method for deterministic finite automata, Moore machines, and Mealy machines, and by deriving new instances of complete test suites for weighted automata and deterministic nominal automata.
elements.sent-2411-006: In conformance testing of deterministic automata, the problem is to check the equivalence of a known specification and a black-box implementation~\cite{DBLP:conf/dagstuhl/testing05 .}
elements.sent-2411-007: This form of testing is widely used, for instance, in practical implementations of automata learning, where one aims to infer an automaton model by systematically interacting with a black-box system~\cite{DBLP:phd/dnb/Isberner15,DBLP:conf/dagstuhl/HowarS16,DBLP:journals/cacm/Vaandrager17 .}
elements.sent-2411-008: In particular, conformance testing is a standard technique to evaluate whether a learned model is correct.
elements.sent-2411-009: In Angluin's minimally adequate teacher (MAT) framework for active automata learning~\cite{DBLP:journals/iandc/Angluin87 , this is referred to as an equivalence query --- which, when the system under learning really is a black-box, can only be implemented through testing.}
elements.sent-2411-010: In a black-box setting, testing can never be exhaustive~\cite{moore1956gedanken : for any (finite) collection of tests, we can construct a faulty implementation that passes all the tests.}
elements.sent-2411-011: However, under certain assumptions on the implementation, it is possible to construct test suites which guarantee equivalence.
elements.sent-2411-012: These test suites are called complete.
elements.sent-2411-013: In particular, an $m$-complete test suite is complete with respect to all implementations with at most $m$ states.
elements.sent-2411-014: The W-method~\cite{vasilevskii1973failure,DBLP:journals/tse/Chow78 is a classical construction of an $m$-complete test suite, which has been extended and improved in numerous ways (see~\cite{DBLP:journals/pieee/LeeY96,DBLP:journals/infsof/DorofeevaEMCY10,moerman2019nominal for an overview).}}
elements.sent-2411-015: State-of-the-art libraries for automata learning~\cite{IsbernerHS15,MuskardinAPPT21 implement various (randomised) versions of these test suites to evaluate the equivalence query.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Active automata learning has been extended from deterministic finite automata~\cite{DBLP:journals/iandc/Angluin87 to a wide range of system types, such as Mealy machines~\cite{MargariaNRS04 , non-deterministic automata~\cite{BolligHKL09 , register automata~\cite{CEGAR12,BolligHLM13,CasselHJS16 , nominal automata~\cite{MoermanS0KS17 , weighted automata~\cite{Bergadano,BalleM15,DBLP:conf/fossacs/HeerdtKR020 , automata over infinite words~\cite{MalerP95,AngluinAF19 , and probabilistic models~\cite{TapplerA0EL21 .}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Moreover, automata learning algorithms have been generalized to the abstract level of category theory~\cite{DBLP:conf/fossacs/BarloccoKR19,DBLP:conf/lics/UrbatS20,DBLP:conf/cmcs/HeerdtSS20,DBLP:conf/csl/ColcombetPS21 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: However, most variants of complete test suites are only developed for Mealy machines and DFAs.
elements.sent-2411-019: While the abstract frameworks for automata learning rely on equivalence queries, a general theory of evaluating these in practice through conformance testing is missing.
elements.sent-2411-020: Developing such a theory is relevant, as evaluating the equivalence query is often the bottleneck in automata learning~\cite{AslamCSB20 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: In this paper, we study conformance testing at the generality of machines in a category, more specifically in monoidal closed categories.
elements.sent-2411-022: This setting goes back to classical work by Arbib and Manes~\cite{arbib1975adjoint , Goguen~\cite{DBLP:journals/jcss/Goguen75 , and Adámek and Trnková~\cite{adamek1990automata .}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: The key feature that makes this setting work for conformance testing is that it allows us to view automata both as algebras and as coalgebras.
elements.sent-2411-024: Our main contribution is to define the W-method and to provide a framework for proving $(m)$-completeness of test suites at the general level of automata in monoidal closed categories.
elements.sent-2411-025: To this end, we generalize part of the bisimulation-based proof presented in~\cite{DBLP:conf/tacas/KrugerJR24 for Mealy machines, which ultimately goes back to~\cite{vasilevskii1973failure,DBLP:journals/tse/Chow78 .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: In particular, our main result allows us to prove completeness under the assumption that the we can reach all states in the implementation, a property guaranteed by the W-method in concrete cases.
elements.sent-2411-027: We instantiate our approach to recover known complete test suites for deterministic finite automata, Mealy machines, and Moore machines.
elements.sent-2411-028: We go on to instantiate it to weighted automata~\cite{DBLP:books/ems/21/DrosteK21 and deterministic nominal automata~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/BojanczykKL14 , providing, to the best of our knowledge, the first account of complete test suites for these systems.}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Our approach is inspired by the categorical automata learning framework of Urbat and Schr\"{o der~\cite{DBLP:conf/lics/UrbatS20 , which similarly relies on the possibility of viewing automata as both algebras and coalgebras in the more general setting of adjoint automata.}}

elements.sent-2411-117: We wish to generalize the W-method to a more general categorical setting.
elements.sent-2411-118: The first hurdle towards achieving this goal is giving definitions of a state cover and of a characterization set.
elements.sent-2411-119: This is challenging because the nature of the two sets is fundamentally different: the state cover concerns reachability of states, whereas the characterization set is about equivalence of states.
elements.sent-2411-120: These two notions are dual to each other~\cite{arbib1975adjoint : the former is an algebraic notion, while the latter is a coalgebraic one.}
elements.sent-2411-121: Thus, we need a framework in which we can use both an algebraic and a coalgebraic characterization of automata.
elements.sent-2411-122: We observe that the setting of automata in monoidal closed categories (called machines by Goguen~\cite{DBLP:journals/jcss/Goguen75 and sequential $\Sigma$-automata by Adámek and Trnková~\cite{adamek1990automata in the Cartesian monoidal case) provides the sweet spot we need.}}

elements.sent-2411-124: It covers many kinds of automata, including DFAs, Moore machines, Mealy machines, linear weighted automata, and deterministic nominal automata.
elements.sent-2411-125: This setting is particularly nice since it allows us to work with concrete descriptions of the initial algebra and of the final coalgebra for the functors describing the algebraic and coalgebraic presentations of automata, respectively.

elements.sent-2411-354: Nominal automata~\cite{DBLP:journals/corr/BojanczykKL14 are a model of computation over potentially infinite alphabets.}
elements.sent-2411-355: They are based on the notion of nominal sets, which are essentially sets with certain symmetries.
elements.sent-2411-356: The idea is that we demand that the transition function of a nominal automaton respect the symmetries of the state space, so that we can represent it via finite manners.
elements.sent-2411-357: In this subsection, we fix a countable set $\atoms$ of atoms.
elements.sent-2411-358: We denote by $\Perm{\atoms}$ the group of finite permutations over $\atoms$ (i.e. bijections $\pi \colon \atoms \to \atoms$ such that $\pi(a) = a$ for all but finitely many $a \in \atoms$).
elements.sent-2411-359: For a $\Perm{\atoms}$-set $X$, we denote the action of $\pi$ on an element $x \in X$ by $\pi \cdot x$.
elements.sent-2411-360: We recall that an equivariant function $f \colon X \to Y$ between $\Perm{\atoms}$-sets satisfies $f(\pi \cdot x) = \pi \cdot f(x)$, an equivariant subset $A \subseteq X$ is such that $x \in A \implies \pi \cdot x \in A$, and an equivariant element $a \in X$ is such that $\{a\}$ is an equivariant subset.
elements.sent-2411-361: For background on nominal sets in general, we refer to~\cite{pitts2013nominal .}
elements.sent-2411-362: Finally, we fix an orbit-finite nominal set $\abcobj$ acting as the alphabet.
elements.sent-2411-363: A deterministic nominal automaton (or DNA for short) is a tuple $(Q, q_0, \delta, F)$, where $Q$ is an orbit-finite nominal set of states, $q_0 \in Q$ is an equivariant initial state, $\delta \colon Q \times \Sigma \to Q$ is an equivariant transition map, and $F \subseteq Q$ is an equivariant subset of final states.
elements.sent-2411-364: For DNA $\aut{A = (Q, q_0, \delta, F)$, we write $q \in \aut{A}$ for $q \in Q$.}
elements.sent-2411-365: DNAs are covered by our framework, since they correspond precisely to automata over $\abcobj$ in the category $\Nom$ of nominal sets and equivariant maps with the cartesian product as monoidal structure, taking the output object $\outobj = \2$, the discrete nominal set with 2 elements.
elements.sent-2411-366: To see this, note that, given a nominal set $Q$, an equivariant element $q_0 \in Q$ corresponds to an equivariant map $\1 \to Q$ (where \1 is the discrete nominal set with 1 element), and that an equivariant subset $F \subseteq Q$ corresponds to an equivariant map $Q \to \2$.
elements.sent-2411-367: We identify DNAs with automata in $\Nom$ via this correspondence.
elements.sent-2411-368: DNAs recognize nominal languages: these are equivariant subsets of $\words$, the nominal set of words over $\abcobj$ with the pointwise group action.

elements.sent-2411-404: We introduced a general framework for studying the W-method and proving completeness of test suites, at the categorical level of automata in monoidal closed categories.
elements.sent-2411-405: Besides recovering basic instances such as DFAs, Mealy machines, and Moore machines, our framework instantiates to complete test suites for the new cases of weighted automata and deterministic nominal automata.
elements.sent-2411-406: There are several open questions and avenues for future work.
elements.sent-2411-407: We have focused on the classical W-method, but it would be useful to also cover refinements thereof such as Wp~\cite{FujiwaraTesting1991 , where distinguishing sequences depend on the state reached, or Hybrid ADS~\cite{moerman2019nominal .}}
elements.sent-2411-408: The latter requires an understanding of adaptive distinguishing sequences at an abstract level, which is of interest on its own.
elements.sent-2411-409: A further direction is to include in our framework a notion of finiteness of test suites using, e.g., finitely presentable objects~\cite{AR94 .}
elements.sent-2411-410: Moreover, a key aspect of the W-method is that the size of the state cover and characterization set are polynomial in the size of the specification, and it would be useful to capture this at a more general level.
elements.sent-2411-411: A possible direction for future work is to move from weighted automata over a field to weighted automata over a semiring, and complement for instance the learning algorithms for weighted automata over the integers proposed in~\cite{DBLP:conf/fossacs/HeerdtKR020,DBLP:journals/pacmpl/BunaMargineanCSW24 .}
elements.sent-2411-412: Instantiating to the Boolean semiring could then connect to more concrete work on complete test suites for non-deterministic automata~\cite{DBLP:conf/hase/PetrenkoY14 .. \bibliographystyle{splncs04 .}}
